ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roasted pork rice as a composite Singaporean dish; mapped to broader pork dish class as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (Pork meat dishes) is the closest defensible category for this composite rice-and-roasted-pork dish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for siu yuk fan.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for roasted pork rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard hawker-centre serving of roasted pork rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice topped with 120–130 g of sliced roasted pork belly and 20–30 g of sauce and garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05100114 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB entry. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted pork belly (NDB 10219) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roasted pork rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (jasmine/cooked) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 25.15 g/100g × 350 g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ 63.4 g available carbs per 350 g serving; GL = 72 × 63.4 / 100 ≈ 45.6, rounded to 45.3 using HPB carbohydrate figure.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 291 mg/100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,019 mg sodium, representing 44% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg, with additional sodium risk from soy-based sauces served alongside.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce, avoid additional soy or dark soy sauce, and limit portion size to no more than half a standard plate to keep per-meal sodium below 500 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72) with 25.15 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 1.22 g dietary fibre/100 g produces a high glycemic load (GL ≈ 45 per standard serving), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People managing Type 2 Diabetes should substitute brown rice or reduce rice portion to half, add non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre, and avoid sweet chilli or hoisin sauces to limit glycemic impact per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Roasted pork belly contributes approximately 3.1 g saturated fat/100 g and the dish provides 8.61 g total fat/100 g; a standard serving delivers roughly 10.8 g saturated fat, approaching the AHA daily limit of 13 g for a 2,000 kcal diet.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request leaner pork cuts such as loin rather than belly, limit portion to half a standard serving, and avoid additional oil-based or sweet sauces to reduce saturated fat and total caloric load per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Combination of refined white rice (pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate) and roasted pork belly (red meat high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat) with no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request leaner pork cuts, substitute brown rice, add steamed vegetables for antioxidants, and reduce soy sauce to lower sodium and overall inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork Rice (烧肉饭, siu yuk fan) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of sliced crispy-skinned roasted pork belly (siu yuk) served over steamed white rice, typically accompanied by a savoury soy-based sauce and sometimes garnished with cucumber slices. The pork belly is marinated with five-spice powder, garlic, soy sauce, and salt, then roasted at high heat to achieve a crackling skin and tender, juicy interior. The dish is a staple at Chinese roast-meat stalls (siu mei stalls) found throughout Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 208 kcal, 8.22 g protein, 8.61 g fat, and 25.15 g carbohydrate with only 1.22 g dietary fibre, reflecting the dominance of white rice as the carbohydrate base. Sodium is 291 mg/100 g, making a standard 350 g serving a moderate-to-high sodium meal. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high-GI white rice base and high glycemic load. It carries moderate cardiovascular concern due to saturated fat from pork belly. Hypertension management requires strict sauce and portion control. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.
common_user_queries: How many calories in siu yuk fan?
Can diabetics eat roasted pork rice?
Is siu yuk rice high in sodium?
Siu yuk fan calories per plate
Is roasted pork rice suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F05100114, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for roasted pork belly as HPB does not separately report this value for the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which is the standard defensible approach for composite rice dishes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.1 g/100 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for roasted pork (NDB 10219) proportionally adjusted for the rice-to-pork ratio in this composite dish; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for F05100114. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean roasted pork rice dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate using cooked white rice (jasmine) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for roasted pork rice as a composite dish exists in approved sources. 4. The dish contains pork and is not halal. 5. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are mapped to broader category anchors as no exact ontology term exists for this composite Singaporean dish.
